title: Latent Tyre-State Features From a Lightweight Sequential Degradation Model
summary: A recent F1 degradation paper argues that tyre wear is better represented as a latent stochastic state with pit-stop resets than by naive rolling averages.
rationale: A recent F1 degradation paper argues that tyre wear is better represented as a latent stochastic state with pit-stop resets than by naive rolling averages. That matches your history: simple rolling pace features were weak, but that does not rule out a better sequential state estimate.
implementation_hint: For each `Year/Race/Driver/Stint`, fit a leakage-safe expanding model using only prior laps to estimate latent pace level, degradation slope, slope acceleration, residual uncertainty, and post-stop warm-up offset. Use simple Kalman/state-space or even recursive linear updates; feed the derived states into the tabular model.
expected_effect: Could recover signal from pace deterioration, warm-up behavior, and uncertainty that raw `LapTime_Delta` and `Cumulative_Degradation` do not isolate well.
risk: Easy to leak if current/future laps are used in the state estimate; overly fancy filtering may overfit given limited per-stint length.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
